name: Paracetamol
uses: Relieves mild to moderate pain (headache, muscle ache, toothache) and reduces fever.
mechanism: Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis in the central nervous system; minimal anti-inflammatory effect.
dosage: Adults: 500–1000 mg every 4–6 hours as needed. Maximum 4000 mg per 24 hours. Do not exceed recommended dose.
sideEffects: Generally well tolerated. Rare: rash, liver damage with overdose, allergic reactions.
interactions: Avoid combining with other products containing paracetamol/acetaminophen. Caution with warfarin (may increase INR). Alcohol increases liver toxicity risk.
contraindications: Severe liver disease, known hypersensitivity to paracetamol.
warnings: Overdose can cause fatal liver failure. Check all cold/flu products for hidden paracetamol content.
foodInstructions: Can be taken with or without food.
storage: Store at room temperature, away from moisture and heat.
pregnancyCategory: Generally considered safe in pregnancy at recommended doses; consult a healthcare provider.

name: Ibuprofen
uses: Reduces pain, inflammation, and fever. Used for headache, dental pain, menstrual cramps, arthritis, and muscle pain.
mechanism: Inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2), reducing prostaglandin production.
dosage: Adults: 200–400 mg every 4–6 hours as needed. Maximum 1200 mg per day OTC; higher doses require medical supervision.
sideEffects: Stomach upset, nausea, heartburn, dizziness. Long-term use: GI bleeding, kidney problems, increased cardiovascular risk.
interactions: Avoid with other NSAIDs (aspirin, naproxen). Increases bleeding risk with anticoagulants. May reduce effectiveness of some blood pressure medications.
contraindications: Active peptic ulcer, severe kidney disease, third trimester of pregnancy, aspirin-sensitive asthma.
warnings: Take with food or milk to reduce stomach irritation. Seek medical help for black stools or severe abdominal pain.
foodInstructions: Take with food or after meals to minimize GI side effects.
pregnancyCategory: Avoid in third trimester; use lowest effective dose in first/second trimester only if advised by a doctor.

name: Metformin
uses: First-line treatment for type 2 diabetes. Helps lower blood sugar by improving insulin sensitivity and reducing glucose production in the liver.
mechanism: Decreases hepatic glucose production, improves peripheral insulin sensitivity, and may reduce intestinal glucose absorption.
dosage: Typical starting dose: 500 mg once or twice daily with meals. Gradually increased to 1500–2000 mg/day in divided doses. Extended-release formulations taken once daily with evening meal.
sideEffects: Common: nausea, diarrhea, stomach upset, metallic taste. Usually improves over time. Rare: lactic acidosis (medical emergency).
interactions: Contrast dye for imaging may require temporary discontinuation. Alcohol increases lactic acidosis risk. Some diuretics may affect blood sugar control.
contraindications: Severe kidney impairment (eGFR below 30), acute heart failure, metabolic acidosis, severe liver disease.
warnings: Stop before scheduled contrast imaging and restart per doctor instructions. Report muscle pain, unusual fatigue, or breathing difficulty immediately.
foodInstructions: Always take with meals to reduce gastrointestinal side effects.
pregnancyCategory: Used in gestational diabetes under medical supervision.

name: Amoxicillin
uses: Treats bacterial infections including respiratory tract infections, ear infections, urinary tract infections, and H. pylori (in combination).
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis, causing cell lysis.
dosage: Adults: 250–500 mg every 8 hours or 500–875 mg every 12 hours depending on infection. Complete the full prescribed course even if symptoms improve.
sideEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, rash. Allergic reactions range from rash to anaphylaxis in penicillin-allergic patients.
interactions: May reduce effectiveness of oral contraceptives (use backup contraception). Probenecid increases amoxicillin levels. Allopurinol may increase rash risk.
contraindications: History of serious penicillin or beta-lactam allergy.
warnings: Do not use for viral infections (cold, flu). Incomplete courses contribute to antibiotic resistance.
foodInstructions: Can be taken with or without food.
storage: Store at room temperature. Refrigerate liquid suspension after reconstitution.

name: Omeprazole
uses: Treats GERD, peptic ulcers, and excessive stomach acid. Also used for H. pylori eradication (with antibiotics).
mechanism: Irreversibly blocks the gastric proton pump (H+/K+ ATPase), reducing stomach acid production.
dosage: Typical: 20–40 mg once daily before breakfast. Duration depends on condition (4–8 weeks for ulcers, longer for GERD maintenance).
sideEffects: Headache, nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain. Long-term use: may affect magnesium, B12 absorption; slight fracture risk with prolonged use.
interactions: May reduce absorption of drugs requiring acid (ketoconazole, iron, some bisphosphonates). Clopidogrel effectiveness may be reduced. Affects metabolism of some drugs via CYP2C19.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to omeprazole or other PPIs.
warnings: Long-term use should be reviewed periodically. Do not stop abruptly without medical advice if used long-term.
foodInstructions: Take 30–60 minutes before the first meal of the day for best effect.
pregnancyCategory: Use only if clearly needed; consult a healthcare provider.
